---
title: "Strange DNS Resolution Problem - 10.04"
date: 2010-05-13
forum: Server Platforms
---

### Post by TheFuturian on 2010-05-13
Hi All,

I am in the process of migrating from a 8.04 server to a flashy new 10.04 box. I have noticed a small change in the method used for dns resolution that I can't seem to get my head around. On both my old and new servers I have the following entries in /etc/resolv.conf:-

```
# Dynamic resolv.conf(5) file for glibc resolver(3) generated by resolvconf(8)
#     DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE BY HAND -- YOUR CHANGES WILL BE OVERWRITTEN
nameserver 10.0.0.1
search domain.local
```

I can ping dc1 or dc1.domain.local without issues on my old server:-

**_Old Server_**
```
admin@Ubuntu-Server-LTS:/etc$ ping dc1
PING dc1.domain.local (10.0.0.1) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from dc1.domain.local (10.0.0.1): icmp_seq=1 ttl=128 time=0.207 ms
```


```
admin@Ubuntu-Server-LTS:/etc$ ping dc1.domain.local
PING dc1.domain.local (10.0.0.1) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from dc1.domain.local (10.0.0.1): icmp_seq=1 ttl=128 time=0.194 ms
```

**_New Server_**
```
admin@Ubuntu-Server-LTS:/etc$ ping dc1
PING dc1.domain.local (10.0.0.1) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from dc1.domain.local (10.0.0.1): icmp_seq=1 ttl=128 time=0.218 ms
```


```
admin@Ubuntu-Server-LTS:/etc$ ping dc1.domain.local
ping: unknown host dc1.domain.local
```

If I use nslookup to resolve dc1.domain.local on my new box I get the expected result:-

```
admin@Ubuntu-Server-LTS:~$ nslookup
> dc1.domain.local
Server:		10.0.0.1
Address:	10.0.0.1#53

Name:	dc1.domain.local
Address: 10.0.0.1
```

I have tried a few bodgit fixes (such as installing/tweaking resolvconf), however had no joy in retaining this functionality. I think it's a useful and intuitive way of performing the resolution, as such it would be nice if I can continue working with both simple and FQDN's on my ubuntu server. If anyone can shed any light on the problem it would be much appreciated :)

---

### Post by TheFuturian on 2010-05-13
Found this old thread:-

_[http://www.uluga.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1110130](http://www.uluga.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1110130)_

Have renamed **/etc/init/avahi-daemon.conf** to **avahi-daemon.disabled**, rebooted and resolution is working as expected on my new server \\:D/

Hopefully it wasn't anything important.. :-\"

---

### Post by CharlesA on 2010-05-13
On the machine that cannot ping by the FQDN, can you run this:

```
hostname -f
```

EDIT: Check [here]("http://en.kioskea.net/faq/739-disabling-the-avahi-daemon") for some info on the Avahi daemon.

### Post by doas777 on 2010-05-13
so, (if you don't mind me butting in), why does avahi never actually work to get you on a network? it always gives me a non-routable 169.254.x.y address that can't talk to anything, and then mucks up all my config files by appending .local to everything. for a long time, in hardy, the avahi daemon broke sudo by reconfiguring the hosts file. 

so what is the point?

---

### Post by arrrghhh on 2010-05-13
It's supposed to enable friendly host names without the need to configure your own DNS server, or individual hosts files for each client.  Zeroconf DNS lookup essentially, see [bonjour](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bonjour_%28software%29).

---

> **arrrghhh said:**

so since it never seems to work, and just causes more serious problems, why is it in there?

### Post by redmk2 on 2010-05-13
> **TheFuturian said:**
> By 'OP' do you mean me? I've never been called that before.. 8-) Its a server install on my new box (x64). I haven't installed ubuntu-desktop, however I did install gnome-core.

I do agree it's a bit of a strange package by the _[description supplied]("http://en.kioskea.net/faq/739-disabling-the-avahi-daemon")_. Assigning an APIPA address is fair enough, but hijacking DNS functionality is just plain rude :P

APIPA is the Windows term for Avahi.  The whole idea is to allow "link local" (e.g. non-routable) IP addressing with no configuration.  No need even for a DHCP server.  Avahi uses mDNS for name services and by default is configured to use the .local domain.  This can be changed.  See [**_here _**]("http://avahi.org/wiki/AvahiAndUnicastDotLocal")for Avahi information.

See [**_here _**]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1473804")for a little more complete discussion.

---
